annotations.label: Greatest common divisor (informal)

anchor.snippet: is the largest positive integer that divides each of the integers

annotations.note: Mathlib's gcd (specializing to Nat.gcd/Int.gcd) formalizes the greatest common divisor as the divisibility-greatest common divisor.

annotations.label: gcd(8, 12) = 4

anchor.snippet: the GCD of 8 and 12 is 4

annotations.note: gcd 8 12 = 4 is a decidable computation of Nat.gcd, provable by `decide`/norm_num via the NormNum.GCD extension.

annotations.label: GCD of integers a and b

anchor.snippet: is the greatest positive integer d such that d is a divisor of both a and b

annotations.note: Int.gcd a b is the natural-number greatest common divisor of two integers, with extensive divisibility API in this module.

annotations.label: GCD when one argument is zero

anchor.snippet: When one of a and b is zero, the GCD is the absolute value of the nonzero integer

annotations.note: gcd a 0 = a holds (for ℤ via Int.gcd this yields a.natAbs = |a|).

annotations.label: Convention gcd(0, 0) = 0

anchor.snippet: gcd(0, 0) is commonly defined as 0

annotations.note: gcd a b = 0 ↔ a = 0 ∧ b = 0, so gcd 0 0 = 0 matches Mathlib's convention.

annotations.label: Common divisors are divisors of the GCD

anchor.snippet: the common divisors of a and b are exactly the divisors of their GCD

annotations.note: a ∣ gcd b c ↔ a ∣ b ∧ a ∣ c states that common divisors are exactly the divisors of the gcd.

annotations.label: GCD of 54 and 24

anchor.snippet: The number 54 can be expressed as a product of two integers in several different ways

annotations.note: gcd 54 24 = 6 is a decidable Nat.gcd computation provable by norm_num/decide.

annotations.label: Coprime numbers

anchor.snippet: Two numbers are called relatively prime, or coprime

annotations.note: Nat.Coprime m n is defined as gcd m n = 1, matching the coprimality definition (general ring version is IsCoprime).

annotations.label: Geometric view of gcd(24, 60)

anchor.snippet: a 24-by-60 rectangular area can be divided into a grid of

annotations.note: The geometric tiling interpretation of the gcd is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reducing fractions with the GCD

anchor.snippet: The greatest common divisor is useful for reducing fractions to the lowest terms

annotations.note: Dividing numerator and denominator by their gcd yields coprime quotients (lowest terms), but reducing fractions is an application rather than a named theorem.

annotations.label: LCM from GCD relation

anchor.snippet: The least common multiple of two integers that are not both zero can be computed from their greatest common divisor

annotations.note: gcd a b * lcm a b = a * b gives lcm = a·b / gcd.

annotations.label: GCD via prime factorizations

anchor.snippet: Greatest common divisors can be computed by determining the prime factorizations of the two numbers and comparing factors

annotations.note: (gcd a b).factorization = a.factorization ⊓ b.factorization, i.e. the gcd takes the minimum prime exponents.

annotations.label: Euclid's subtraction principle

anchor.snippet: the common divisors of a and b are the same as the common divisors of a – b and b

annotations.note: gcd of (a - b) and b equals gcd a b, the subtraction step of Euclid's algorithm.

annotations.label: Euclidean algorithm with remainder

anchor.snippet: a variant in which the difference of the two numbers a and b is replaced by the remainder

annotations.note: gcd a b = gcd (b % a) a is exactly the remainder form of the Euclidean algorithm (Nat.gcd is defined by this recursion).

annotations.label: Binary GCD algorithm

anchor.snippet: The binary GCD algorithm is a variant of Euclid's algorithm that is specially adapted to the binary representation of the numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib has no binary (Stein's) GCD algorithm implementation.

annotations.label: Binary GCD computational complexity

anchor.snippet: Its computational complexity is

annotations.note: Computational complexity of the binary GCD algorithm is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lehmer's GCD algorithm

anchor.snippet: Lehmer's algorithm is based on the observation that the initial quotients

annotations.note: Lehmer's GCD algorithm is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: GCD via LCM

anchor.snippet: the greatest common divisor of a and b can be computed by using least common multiple

annotations.note: gcd a b * lcm a b = a * b lets gcd be recovered from lcm as a·b / lcm.

annotations.label: GCD via Thomae's function

anchor.snippet: Using Thomae's function f

annotations.note: The Thomae-function formula for gcd is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Slavin's formula for odd a

anchor.snippet: Keith Slavin has shown that for odd a ≥ 1

annotations.note: Slavin's gcd formula is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Schramm's entire-function formula

anchor.snippet: Wolfgang Schramm has shown that

annotations.note: Schramm's entire-function formula for gcd is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euclidean GCD complexity O(n^2)

anchor.snippet: the computation of the greatest common divisor of two integers of at most n bits is

annotations.note: Bit-complexity bounds for GCD computation are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fast-multiplication GCD complexity

anchor.snippet: the fastest known algorithm for greatest common divisor has a complexity

annotations.note: GCD complexity with fast multiplication is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: GCD solvable in quasilinear time

anchor.snippet: belongs thus to the class of problems solvable in quasilinear time

annotations.note: Complexity-class statements about GCD are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chor–Goldreich parallel algorithm

anchor.snippet: the fastest known deterministic algorithm is by Chor and Goldreich

annotations.note: The Chor–Goldreich parallel GCD algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: gcd(a, a) = a

anchor.snippet: For every positive integer

annotations.note: gcd a a = a (Nat.gcd_self gives the natural-number special case).

annotations.label: Common divisor divides the GCD

anchor.snippet: Every common divisor of a and b is a divisor of

annotations.note: c ∣ gcd a b ↔ c ∣ a ∧ c ∣ b, so every common divisor divides the gcd (also Nat.gcd_greatest).

annotations.label: Bézout's identity

anchor.snippet: may be defined alternatively and equivalently as the smallest positive integer

annotations.note: gcd a b = a * gcdA a b + b * gcdB a b is Bézout's identity expressing the gcd as an integer combination.

annotations.label: gcd(a, 0) = |a|

anchor.snippet: since any number is a divisor of 0

annotations.note: gcd a 0 = a (Int.gcd a 0 = a.natAbs = |a|), since everything divides 0.

annotations.label: Euclid's lemma consequence

anchor.snippet: If a divides the product

annotations.note: If a is coprime to b and a ∣ b·c then a ∣ c (Nat.Coprime.dvd_of_dvd_mul_left gives the integer case).

annotations.label: GCD scaling by m

anchor.snippet: If m is a positive integer, then

annotations.note: gcd (m*a) (m*b) = m * gcd a b expresses scaling of the gcd by a common factor.

annotations.label: GCD invariance under translation

anchor.snippet: If m is any integer, then

annotations.note: gcd lemmas like gcd (a + m*b) b = gcd a b formalize invariance of the gcd under adding a multiple.

annotations.label: GCD of quotients by common divisor

anchor.snippet: If m is a positive common divisor of

annotations.note: For m ∣ a and m ∣ b, gcd (a/m) (b/m) = gcd a b / m.

annotations.label: Quotients by GCD are coprime

anchor.snippet: If gcd( a , b ) = d , then gcd( a / d , b / d ) = 1

annotations.note: Dividing a and b by their gcd produces coprime quotients.

annotations.label: GCD is commutative

anchor.snippet: The GCD is a commutative function

annotations.note: gcd a b = gcd b a in any NormalizedGCDMonoid (Nat.gcd_comm is the special case).

annotations.label: GCD is associative

anchor.snippet: The GCD is an associative function

annotations.note: gcd (gcd m n) k = gcd m (gcd n k) in any NormalizedGCDMonoid.

annotations.label: GCD is multiplicative

anchor.snippet: The GCD is a multiplicative function in the following sense

annotations.note: For coprime m, n, gcd k (m*n) = gcd k m * gcd k n, the multiplicative property of the gcd.

annotations.label: GCD–LCM product relation

anchor.snippet: is closely related to the least common multiple

annotations.note: gcd a b * lcm a b is associated to a * b (Nat.gcd_mul_lcm gives equality on ℕ).

annotations.label: GCD–LCM distributivity

anchor.snippet: The following versions of distributivity hold true

annotations.note: Full gcd/lcm distributive laws gcd a (lcm b c) = lcm (gcd a b) (gcd a c) are not stated as named lemmas, though the gcd/lcm and dvd infrastructure exists.

annotations.label: GCD from prime factorizations

anchor.snippet: If we have the unique prime factorizations of

annotations.note: (gcd a b).factorization = a.factorization ⊓ b.factorization, the minimum-exponent product formula.

annotations.label: Naturals form a distributive lattice

anchor.snippet: the natural numbers become a complete distributive lattice

annotations.note: Mathlib has no complete-distributive-lattice instance on ℕ ordered by divisibility with gcd as meet and lcm as join.

annotations.label: GCD as lattice points on a segment

anchor.snippet: In a Cartesian coordinate system

annotations.note: The count of lattice points on the segment from origin to (a,b) being gcd(a,b) is not formalized.

annotations.label: GCD of n^a − 1 and n^b − 1

anchor.snippet: provable by considering the Euclidean algorithm in base

annotations.note: The identity gcd(n^a−1, n^b−1) = n^gcd(a,b)−1 is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Identity with Euler's totient

anchor.snippet: An identity involving Euler's totient function

annotations.note: φ(gcd a b) * φ(a*b) = φ a * φ b * gcd a b, equivalent to the Wikipedia totient–gcd identity.

annotations.label: Pillai's GCD summatory function

anchor.snippet: GCD Summatory function (Pillai's arithmetical function)

annotations.note: Pillai's gcd-summatory function and its formula are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nymann's coprimality probability

anchor.snippet: In 1972, James E. Nymann showed that k integers, chosen independently and uniformly

annotations.note: The probability 1/ζ(k) that k random integers are coprime is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probability GCD equals d

anchor.snippet: This result was extended in 1987 to show that the probability that k random integers

annotations.note: The asymptotic probability that gcd of k random integers equals d is not formalized.

annotations.label: Expected GCD undefined for k = 2

anchor.snippet: the expected value of the greatest common divisor function can be seen (informally) to not exist

annotations.note: Statements about the (non)existence of the expected gcd are not formalized.

annotations.label: Expected GCD well-defined for k ≥ 3

anchor.snippet: the expected value is well-defined

annotations.note: Well-definedness of the expected gcd for k ≥ 3 is not formalized.

annotations.label: GCD in a commutative ring

anchor.snippet: The notion of greatest common divisor can more generally be defined for elements of an arbitrary commutative ring

annotations.note: GCDMonoid axiomatizes gcd on a CommMonoidWithZero, the general-ring notion of greatest common divisor.

annotations.label: Common divisor in a ring

anchor.snippet: is called a common divisor of a and b if it divides both

annotations.note: A common divisor is just c ∣ a ∧ c ∣ b (expressed via Dvd); there is no dedicated named predicate, but dvd_gcd_iff characterizes such divisors.

annotations.label: Greatest common divisor in a ring

anchor.snippet: then d is called a greatest common divisor of a and b

annotations.note: GCDMonoid.gcd with gcd_dvd_left/right and dvd_gcd captures d being a common divisor that every common divisor divides.

annotations.label: GCDs are associate in integral domains

anchor.snippet: then any two GCDs of a and b must be associate elements

annotations.note: Mathlib's gcd is canonical and unique up to associates (gcd_greatest_associated), but there is no standalone theorem that any two abstract gcds are associate.

annotations.label: GCD existence not assured

anchor.snippet: Existence of a GCD is not assured in arbitrary integral domains

annotations.note: No Mathlib theorem asserts that GCDs can fail to exist in a general integral domain.

annotations.label: GCD exists in UFD / GCD domain

anchor.snippet: if R is a unique factorization domain or any other GCD domain , then any two elements have a GCD

annotations.note: Any UniqueFactorizationMonoid admits a GCDMonoid structure, so any two elements have a gcd.

annotations.label: GCD via Euclidean algorithm in Euclidean domains

anchor.snippet: If R is a Euclidean domain in which euclidean division is given algorithmically

annotations.note: EuclideanDomain.gcd is the algorithmic gcd, and EuclideanDomain.gcdMonoid gives the corresponding GCDMonoid instance.

annotations.label: Integral domain with no GCD

anchor.snippet: The following is an example of an integral domain with two elements that do not have a GCD

annotations.note: A worked example of an integral domain whose two elements have no gcd is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: GCD as principal ideal generator in a PID

anchor.snippet: In a ring all of whose ideals are principal

annotations.note: span_gcd shows Ideal.span {gcd x y} = Ideal.span {x, y}, i.e. gcd generates the ideal generated by a and b.
